topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The delivery man, a young man with an overly bright smile, placed the bouquet on the chipped ceramic table. They were vibrant sunflowers, their heads heavy with pollen, and a few drooping lavender sprigs. I didn't move to look at the accompanying paper; there was nothing there. Just the blooms, a sudden splash of aggressive yellow against the muted grey of my apartment. My shoulders slumped further, the tension in my neck tightening as I took in the unsolicited gesture. I just stared at the arrangement, a dull ache settling deep in my chest. I picked up a stray petal, the texture dry beneath my fingertips, and let it fall onto the worn fabric of my jeans.

[story 2]

When the doorbell chimed, I barely registered it. I was slumped on the sofa, the remote control slipping from my numb fingers as I stared blankly at the flickering television screen. Someone was standing there, holding a vase overflowing with roses—deep crimson and impossibly velvety. I didn't bother to stand up. My eyes, heavy-lidded and unfocused, tracked the movement of the flowers as they were set down on the coffee table. A small, involuntary sigh escaped my lips, a sound more like air escaping a punctured tire. I didn't reach for them. I just watched the vibrant colors sit there, an unwelcome intrusion into the quiet exhaustion that had settled over me like a thick blanket.

[story 3]

The package arrived late, tucked into the mail slot. Inside, nestled in tissue paper, were lilies—stiff, pristine white, demanding attention. I shuffled to the entryway, my movements sluggish, every joint protesting the effort. I picked up the arrangement. It felt cold and heavy in my hands. I turned it over, searching for any inscription, any note to explain the sudden burst of cheer. Finding none, I let the flowers drop back into the box, the sound muffled by the cardboard. I leaned against the doorframe, closing my eyes for a long moment, letting the stillness of the silence wash over the residual fatigue clinging to my limbs.
